 {"id":"bd-4q8","title":"bd cleanup --hard should skip tombstone creation for true permanent deletion","description":"## Problem

When using bd cleanup --hard --older-than N --force, the command:
1. Deletes closed issues older than N days (converting them to tombstones with NOW timestamp)
2. Then tries to prune tombstones older than N days (finds none because they were just created)

This leaves the database bloated with fresh tombstones that will not be pruned.

## Expected Behavior

In --hard mode, the deletion should be permanent without creating tombstones, since the user explicitly requested bypassing sync safety.

## Workaround

Manually delete from database: sqlite3 .beads/beads.db 'DELETE FROM issues WHERE status=tombstone'

## Fix Options

1. In --hard mode, use a different delete path that does not create tombstones
2. After deleting, immediately prune the just-created tombstones regardless of age
3. Pass a skip_tombstone flag to the delete operation

Option 1 is cleanest - --hard should mean permanent delete without tombstone.","status":"tombstone","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-16T01:33:36.580657-08:00","updated_at":"2025-12-17T16:11:17.070763-08:00","deleted_at":"2025-12-17T16:11:17.070763-08:00","deleted_by":"batch delete","delete_reason":"batch delete","original_type":"bug"}

### 3. Create formatter (cmd/bd/doctor/format.go)

```go
type Formatter struct {
    verbose bool
    noColor bool
}

func (f *Formatter) Format(results []CheckResult) string {
    var buf strings.Builder
    
    // Count by status
    passed, warnings, errors := countByStatus(results)
    
    // Header
    buf.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("bd doctor v%s\n\n", version.Version))
    buf.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("Summary: %d passed, %d warnings, %d errors\n\n", 
        passed, warnings, errors))
    
    // Errors section (always show)
    if errors > 0 {
        f.writeSection(&buf, "✗ Errors", filterByStatus(results, StatusError))
    }
    
    // Warnings section (always show)
    if warnings > 0 {
        f.writeSection(&buf, "⚠ Warnings", filterByStatus(results, StatusWarning))
    }
    
    // Passed section (only with --verbose)
    if f.verbose && passed > 0 {
        f.writePassedSection(&buf, filterByStatus(results, StatusPassed))
    } else if passed > 0 {
        buf.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("✓ Passed (%d)  [use --verbose to show details]\n", passed))
    }
    
    return buf.String()
}

func (f *Formatter) writeSection(buf *strings.Builder, title string, results []CheckResult) {
    buf.WriteString("─────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
    buf.WriteString(title + "\n")
    buf.WriteString("─────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n")
    
    for _, r := range results {
        buf.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("[%s] %s\n", r.CheckName, r.Message))
        if r.Details != "" {
            buf.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("  %s\n", r.Details))
        }
        if r.Fix != "" {
            buf.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("  Fix: %s\n", r.Fix))
        }
        buf.WriteString("\n")
    }
}

func (f *Formatter) writePassedSection(buf *strings.Builder, results []CheckResult) {
    // Group by category
    byCategory := groupByCategory(results)
    
    buf.WriteString("─────────────────────────────────────────────────\n")
    buf.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("✓ Passed (%d)\n", len(results)))
    buf.WriteString("─────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n")
    
    for _, cat := range categoryOrder {
        if checks, ok := byCategory[cat]; ok {
            buf.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("  %s\n", cat))
            for _, r := range checks {
                buf.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("    ✓ %s\n", r.Message))
            }
            buf.WriteString("\n")
        }
    }
}
```

### 4. Update run function

```go
func runDoctor(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
    verbose, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("verbose")
    noColor, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("no-color")
    
    results := runAllChecks()
    
    formatter := &Formatter{verbose: verbose, noColor: noColor}
    fmt.Print(formatter.Format(results))
    
    // Exit code based on results
    if hasErrors(results) {
        os.Exit(1)
    }
}
```

 {"id":"bd-4ri","title":"Fix TestFallbackToDirectModeEnablesFlush deadlock causing 10min test timeout","description":"## Problem

TestFallbackToDirectModeEnablesFlush in direct_mode_test.go deadlocks for 9m59s before timing out, causing the entire test suite to take 10+ minutes instead of <10 seconds.

## Root Cause

Database lock contention between test cleanup and flushToJSONL():
- Test cleanup (line 36) tries to close DB via defer
- flushToJSONL() (line 132) is still accessing DB
- Results in deadlock: database/sql.(*DB).Close() waits for mutex while GetJSONLFileHash() holds it

## Stack Trace Evidence

```
goroutine 512 [sync.Mutex.Lock, 9 minutes]:
database/sql.(*DB).Close(0x14000643790)
  .../database/sql/sql.go:927 +0x84
github.com/steveyegge/beads/cmd/bd.TestFallbackToDirectModeEnablesFlush.func1()
  .../direct_mode_test.go:36 +0xf4

While goroutine running flushToJSONL() holds DB connection via GetJSONLFileHash()
```

## Impact

- Test suite: 10+ minutes → should be <10 seconds
- ALL other tests pass in ~4 seconds
- This ONE test accounts for 99.9% of test runtime

## Related

This is the EXACT same issue documented in MAIN_TEST_REFACTOR_NOTES.md for why main_test.go refactoring was deferred - global state manipulation + DB cleanup = deadlock.

## Fix Approaches

1. **Add proper cleanup sequencing** - stop flush goroutines BEFORE closing DB
2. **Use test-specific DB lifecycle** - ensure flush completes before cleanup
3. **Mock the flush mechanism** - avoid real DB for testing this code path  
4. **Add explicit timeout handling** - fail fast with clear error instead of hanging

## Files

- cmd/bd/direct_mode_test.go:36-132
- cmd/bd/autoflush.go:353 (validateJSONLIntegrity)
- cmd/bd/autoflush.go:508 (flushToJSONLWithState)

## Acceptance

 {"id":"bd-g4b4","title":"bd close hooks: context check and notifications","description":"Add hook system to bd close for notifications and custom actions.

## Scope (MVP)

Implement **command hooks only** for bd close. Deferred: notify, webhook types.

## Implementation

### 1. Config Schema

Add to internal/configfile/config.go:

```go
type HooksConfig struct {
    OnClose []HookEntry `yaml:"on_close,omitempty"`
}

type HookEntry struct {
    Command string `yaml:"command"`           // Shell command to run
    Name    string `yaml:"name,omitempty"`    // Optional display name
}
```

Add `Hooks HooksConfig` field to Config struct.

### 2. Hook Execution

Create internal/hooks/close_hooks.go:

```go
func RunCloseHooks(ctx context.Context, cfg *configfile.Config, issue *types.Issue) error {
    for _, hook := range cfg.Hooks.OnClose {
        cmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, "sh", "-c", hook.Command)
        cmd.Env = append(os.Environ(),
            "BEAD_ID="+issue.ID,
            "BEAD_TITLE="+issue.Title,
            "BEAD_TYPE="+string(issue.IssueType),
            "BEAD_PRIORITY="+strconv.Itoa(issue.Priority),
            "BEAD_CLOSE_REASON="+issue.CloseReason,
        )
        cmd.Stdout = os.Stdout
        cmd.Stderr = os.Stderr
        if err := cmd.Run(); err \!= nil {
            // Log warning but dont fail the close
            fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "Warning: close hook %q failed: %v\n", hook.Name, err)
        }
    }
    return nil
}
```

### 3. Integration Point

In cmd/bd/close.go, after successful close:

```go
// Run close hooks
if cfg := configfile.Load(); cfg \!= nil {
    hooks.RunCloseHooks(ctx, cfg, closedIssue)
}
```

### 4. Example Config

```yaml
# .beads/config.yaml
hooks:
  on_close:
    - name: show-next
      command: bd ready --limit 1
    - name: context-check  
      command: echo "Issue $BEAD_ID closed. Check context if nearing limit."
```

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| BEAD_ID | Issue ID (e.g., bd-abc1) |
| BEAD_TITLE | Issue title |
| BEAD_TYPE | Issue type (task, bug, feature, etc.) |
| BEAD_PRIORITY | Priority (0-4) |
| BEAD_CLOSE_REASON | Close reason if provided |

GH#604 raised concerns about bd onboard bloating AGENTS.md with ~100+ lines of static instructions that:
- Load every session whether beads is being used or not
- Get stale when bd upgrades
- Waste tokens

## Solution
Simplify `bd onboard` to output a minimal snippet (~2 lines) that points to `bd prime`:

```markdown
## Issue Tracking
This project uses beads (`bd`) for issue tracking.
Run `bd prime` for workflow context, or hooks auto-inject it.
```

## Rationale
- `bd prime` is dynamic, concise (~80 lines), and always matches installed bd version
- Hooks already auto-inject `bd prime` at session start when .beads/ detected
- AGENTS.md only needs to mention beads exists, not contain full instructions

## Implementation
1. Update `cmd/bd/onboard.go` to output minimal snippet
2. Keep `--output` flag for BD_GUIDE.md generation (may still be useful)
3.  {"id":"bd-iq7n","title":"Audit and fix JSONL filename mismatches across all repo clones","description":"## Problem

Multiple clones of repos are configured with different JSONL filenames (issues.jsonl vs beads.jsonl), causing:
1. JSONL files to be resurrected after deletion (one clone pushes issues.jsonl, another pushes beads.jsonl)
2. Agents unable to see issues filed by other agents after sync
3. Merge conflicts and data inconsistencies

## Root Cause

When repos were "bd doctored" or initialized at different times, some got issues.jsonl (old default) and others got beads.jsonl (Beads repo specific). These clones push their respective files, creating duplicates.

## Task

Scan all repo clones under ~/src/ (1-2 levels deep) and standardize their JSONL configuration.

### Step 1: Find all beads-enabled repos

```bash
# Find all directories named 'beads' at levels 1-2 under ~/src/
find ~/src -maxdepth 2 -type d -name beads
```

### Step 2: For each repo found, check configuration

For each directory from Step 1, check:
- Does `.beads/metadata.json` exist?
- What is the `jsonl_export` value?
- What JSONL files actually exist in `.beads/`?
- Are there multiple JSONL files (problem!)?

### Step 3: Create audit report

Generate a report showing:
```
Repo Path                           | Config        | Actual Files           | Status
----------------------------------- | ------------- | ---------------------- | --------
~/src/beads                         | beads.jsonl   | beads.jsonl            | OK
~/src/dave/beads                    | issues.jsonl  | issues.jsonl           | MISMATCH
~/src/emma/beads                    | issues.jsonl  | issues.jsonl, beads.jsonl | DUPLICATE!
```

### Step 4: Determine canonical name for each repo

For repos that are the SAME git repository (check `git remote -v`):
- Group them together
- Determine which JSONL filename should be canonical (majority wins, or beads.jsonl for the beads repo itself)
- List which clones need to be updated

### Step 5: Generate fix script

Create a script that for each mismatched clone:
1. Updates `.beads/metadata.json` to use the canonical name
2. If JSONL file needs renaming: `git mv .beads/old.jsonl .beads/new.jsonl`
3. Removes any duplicate JSONL files: `git rm .beads/duplicate.jsonl`
4. Commits the change
5. Syncs: `bd sync`

### Expected Output

1. Audit report showing all repos and their config status
2. List of repos grouped by git remote (same repository)
3. Fix script or manual instructions for standardizing each repo
4. Verification that after fixes, all clones of the same repo use the same JSONL filename

### Edge Cases

- Handle repos without metadata.json (use default discovery)
- Handle repos with no git remote (standalone/local)
- Handle repos that are not git repositories
- Don't modify repos with uncommitted changes (warn instead)

### Success Criteria

- All clones of the same git repository use the same JSONL filename
- No duplicate JSONL files in any repo
- All configurations documented in metadata.json
- bd doctor passes on all repos","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-11-21T23:58:35.044762-08:00","updated_at":"2025-12-17T23:13:40.531403-08:00","closed_at":"2025-12-17T16:50:59.510972-08:00"}
 {"id":"bd-is6m","title":"Add gate checking to Deacon patrol loop","description":"Integrate gate checking into Deacon's patrol cycle.

## Patrol Integration
```go
func (d *Deacon) checkGates(ctx context.Context) {
    gates, _ := d.store.ListOpenGates(ctx)
    
    for _, gate := range gates {
        // Check timeout
        if time.Since(gate.CreatedAt) > gate.Timeout {
            d.notifyWaiters(gate, "timeout")
            d.closeGate(gate, "timed out")
            continue
        }
        
        // Check condition
        if d.checkCondition(gate.AwaitType, gate.AwaitID) {
            d.notifyWaiters(gate, "cleared")
            d.closeGate(gate, "condition met")
        }
    }
}
```

```bash
bd init
```


### 2. Create fix registry (cmd/bd/doctor/fix/registry.go)
```go
package fix

// Fixer can automatically repair an issue
type Fixer interface {
    // CanFix returns true if this fixer handles the given check name
    CanFix(checkName string) bool
    // Fix attempts to repair the issue, returns error if failed
    Fix(ctx context.Context, issue *CheckResult) error
    // Description returns human-readable description of what will be fixed
    Description() string
}

var registry []Fixer

func Register(f Fixer) {
    registry = append(registry, f)
}

func GetFixer(checkName string) Fixer {
    for _, f := range registry {
        if f.CanFix(checkName) {
            return f
        }
    }
    return nil
}
```

### 4. Update doctor run logic

```go
func runDoctor(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
    issues := runAllChecks()
    
    if \!fixFlag {
        // Existing behavior - just display issues
        displayIssues(issues)
        return
    }
    
    // Collect fixable issues
    var fixable []FixableIssue
    for _, issue := range issues {
        if fixer := fix.GetFixer(issue.CheckName); fixer \!= nil {
            fixable = append(fixable, FixableIssue{issue, fixer})
        }
    }
    
    if len(fixable) == 0 {
        fmt.Println("No automatically fixable issues found")
        return
    }
    
    // Show what will be fixed
    fmt.Printf("Found %d fixable issues:\n", len(fixable))
    for i, f := range fixable {
        fmt.Printf("  %d. [%s] %s\n", i+1, f.Issue.CheckName, f.Fixer.Description())
    }
    
    // Confirm unless --yes
    if \!yesFlag {
        fmt.Print("\nProceed with fixes? [Y/n] ")
        // ... read confirmation
    }
    
    // Apply fixes
    for _, f := range fixable {
        fmt.Printf("Fixing %s... ", f.Issue.CheckName)
        if err := f.Fixer.Fix(ctx, f.Issue); err \!= nil {
            fmt.Printf("FAILED: %v\n", err)
        } else {
            fmt.Println("OK")
        }
    }
}
```

### 4. Update doctor run logic

```go
func runDoctor(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
    issues := runAllChecks()
    
    if \!fixFlag {
        // Existing behavior - just display issues
        displayIssues(issues)
        return
    }
    
    // Collect fixable issues
    var fixable []FixableIssue
    for _, issue := range issues {
        if fixer := fix.GetFixer(issue.CheckName); fixer \!= nil {
            fixable = append(fixable, FixableIssue{issue, fixer})
        }
    }
    
    if len(fixable) == 0 {
        fmt.Println("No automatically fixable issues found")
        return
    }
    
    // Show what will be fixed
    fmt.Printf("Found %d fixable issues:\n", len(fixable))
    for i, f := range fixable {
        fmt.Printf("  %d. [%s] %s\n", i+1, f.Issue.CheckName, f.Fixer.Description())
    }
    
    // Confirm unless --yes
    if \!yesFlag {
        fmt.Print("\nProceed with fixes? [Y/n] ")
        // ... read confirmation
    }
    
    // Apply fixes
    for _, f := range fixable {
        fmt.Printf("Fixing %s... ", f.Issue.CheckName)
        if err := f.Fixer.Fix(ctx, f.Issue); err \!= nil {
            fmt.Printf("FAILED: %v\n", err)
        } else {
            fmt.Println("OK")
        }
    }
}
```
